Project.title: Project
Project.description: Represents a client project for system deployment, containing overall project details and branding.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Project entity.

userId.description: Reference to the User who owns or created this project. (Relationship: User 1:N Project)

clientName.description: The name of the client company (e.g., 'Bombril', 'BIC').

clientLogo.description: URL to the client's logo image.

clientColor.description: Primary brand color of the client, used for dynamic theming (e.g., '#FF0000').

clientColorSecondary.description: Secondary brand color of the client, used for dynamic theming.

mascotImageUrl.description: URL to an image of the Sellers Pulse mascot customized with the client's branding.

startDate.description: The official start date of the project.

endDate.description: The official estimated end date of the project.

currentPhaseId.description: Reference to the currently active Phase for this project. (Relationship: Project 1:1 Phase)

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the project was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the project was last updated.

Phase.title: Phase
Phase.description: Represents a specific stage or milestone within a project, part of a standard project workflow.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Phase entity.

projectId.description: Reference to the Project this phase belongs to. (Relationship: Project 1:N Phase)

name.description: The descriptive name of the phase (e.g., 'Reunião de Boas-Vindas').

order.description: The sequential order of the phase within the project workflow.

startDate.description: The scheduled start date for this phase.

endDate.description: The scheduled end date for this phase.

status.description: The current status of the phase.

color.description: A color associated with the phase, potentially inherited from the client's brand color.

WeeklyUpdate.title: WeeklyUpdate
WeeklyUpdate.description: Captures weekly progress and key metrics for a project, used for reporting and presentations.

id.description: Unique identifier for the WeeklyUpdate entity.

projectId.description: Reference to the Project this weekly update belongs to. (Relationship: Project 1:N WeeklyUpdate)

weekNumber.description: The sequential week number within the project's lifecycle for this update.

date.description: The date corresponding to this weekly update.

distributorsTotal.description: Total number of distributors associated with the project.

distributorsPending.description: Number of distributors whose integration is pending.

distributorsBlocked.description: Number of distributors whose integration is blocked.

highlights.description: A list of key achievements or positive developments for the week.

blockers.description: A list of obstacles or issues encountered during the week.

nextSteps.description: A list of planned actions for the upcoming period.

aiSummary.description: An AI-generated summary of the weekly update content.

definition.description: Top-level collection for client projects. Each project is owned by a specific user identified by the 'userId' field. This field acts as the primary owner identifier for authorization.

params.name: projectId
params.description: The unique identifier for a client project.

definition.description: Subcollection for phases within a project. Each phase document includes a denormalized 'ownerId' field (copied from the parent project's 'userId') to enable authorization independence and simplified security rules.

params.name: projectId
params.description: The unique identifier of the parent project.

params.name: phaseId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific phase within a project.

definition.description: Subcollection for weekly progress updates of a project. Each weekly update document includes a denormalized 'ownerId' field (copied from the parent project's 'userId') for authorization independence and simplified security rules.

params.name: projectId
params.description: The unique identifier of the parent project.

params.name: weeklyUpdateId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific weekly update within a project.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed to be secure, scalable, and debuggable, adhering strictly to the provided core design principles and strategy mandates. The primary strategy employed is **Authorization Independence via Denormalization** (Mandate A).

1.  **Authorization Independence (CRITICAL) & Denormalization:**
    *   The `Project` entity is stored in the top-level `/projects` collection and includes a `userId` field, which acts as the owner identifier. This is already part of its schema. All access to a project will be gated by this `userId`.
    *   For subcollections (`phases` and `weeklyUpdates`), which logically belong to a specific project and thus implicitly to the project's owner, the `ownerId` (a copy of the parent `Project`'s `userId`) will be denormalized and stored directly within each `Phase` and `WeeklyUpdate` document. This is crucial for eliminating `get()` calls in Firestore Security Rules. Rules for `/projects/{projectId}/phases/{phaseId}` or `/projects/{projectId}/weeklyUpdates/{weeklyUpdateId}` can directly check `resource.data.ownerId == request.auth.uid`, ensuring atomic and independent authorization checks without needing to fetch the parent `Project` document. This greatly simplifies rule logic, improves performance, and prevents issues with transactional integrity.

2.  **QAPs (Queries as Authorization Primitives):**
    *   **`/projects` Collection:** For listing projects, the `projects` collection allows users to perform a query like `db.collection('projects').where('userId', '==', auth.uid)`. Security rules can then permit reads only if `request.auth.uid == resource.data.userId`. This ensures that `list` operations only return projects owned by the authenticated user, making the query itself the primitive for authorization without relying on server-side filters. This is a highly efficient and secure pattern.
    *   **Subcollections (`/projects/{projectId}/phases` and `/projects/{projectId}/weeklyUpdates`):** Because the `ownerId` is denormalized into each document, `list` queries on these subcollections are also QAP-compliant. A user can only access these subcollections if they know the `projectId` of an owned project. The security rules for these paths will check `resource.data.ownerId == request.auth.uid` for every document in the result set, automatically filtering out any documents that might somehow exist for projects not owned by the requesting user, even if the `projectId` in the path matches.

3.  **Structural Segregation & Access Modeling:**
    *   All documents within `/projects` represent user-owned projects, sharing a homogeneous security posture (owner-access). Similarly, `phases` and `weeklyUpdates` documents within a project subcollection share the security posture of being tied to that project's owner.
    *   The design uses a clear **Hierarchical Path for User-Owned Data** (`/projects/{projectId}`) and **Continues Hierarchy for Nested Data** (`/projects/{projectId}/phases/{phaseId}`, `/projects/{projectId}/weeklyUpdates/{weeklyUpdateId}`). This provides clear ownership and logical grouping, simplifying rule writing.

4.  **Clarity & Predictability:**
    *   Consistent naming conventions are used (e.g., `projectId`, `phaseId`, `weeklyUpdateId`). Wildcards are descriptive. The `status` field in `Phase` explicitly models its state.

This robust structure minimizes the potential for security vulnerabilities, simplifies Firebase Security Rules, and optimizes for scalable data access patterns.
